subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Salaam and welcome, young scientist! In this unit you are going to become a materials detective. Every single day, all around you, materials are being mixed together and pulled apart again. When your mother stirs sugar into a hot glass of shaah (tea), she is mixing materials. When you shake sand off your feet after playing at the Indian Ocean beach in Mombasa or Kismaayo, you are separating materials. When Grandmother picks the tiny stones out of the bariis (rice) before cooking, she is separating a mixture. Science is not only found in books and laboratories. It is happening in your own kitchen, at the well, and on the shore.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 3 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Explain what a mixture is and give examples of mixtures from your own home.
Describe what it means for a material to dissolve, and use the word solution correctly.
Predict and test which materials dissolve in water and which do not.
Name and describe four ways to separate mixtures: sieving, filtering, evaporation, and letting things settle (decanting).
Choose the best separation method for a given mixture and explain why it works.
Plan and carry out a fair test, changing only one thing at a time.
Record what you observe carefully and honestly, and draw a sensible conclusion.
Here are the important words for this unit. Read them slowly. You will meet each one again inside the lessons, so do not worry about memorising them all at once.

concepts.title: In this unit you will learn what a mixture is, what it…
concepts.explanation: In this unit you will learn what a mixture is, what it means for something to dissolve, why some things dissolve in water and some do not, and the clever ways we can separate a mixture back into its parts. You will do safe experiments with things already in your home, and your AI Tutor is ready to help you any time you have a question, day or night. Let us begin!

concepts.title: Think about a normal morning in your home. Someone lights…
concepts.explanation: Think about a normal morning in your home. Someone lights the charcoal stove. Water is poured into a pot to boil. Flour and water are stirred together to make canjeero batter. Salt is added to the goat-meat stew. Tea leaves, sugar, and milk swirl together in the pot. In just one hour of the morning, your family has already mixed and separated many materials without even thinking of it as science. But it is science, and understanding it makes you a smarter, more careful person.
concepts.example: Why does this matter for your real life? When you understand which materials dissolve, you understand why sugar sweetens your whole cup of tea but sand never would. When you understand separating, you understand how the…

concepts.title: Why does this matter for your real life? When you…
concepts.explanation: Why does this matter for your real life? When you understand which materials dissolve, you understand why sugar sweetens your whole cup of tea but sand never would. When you understand separating, you understand how the muddy water from the well becomes clear enough to be safe after it settles and is filtered and boiled. When you understand mixtures, you understand cooking, cleaning, and even keeping your family healthy. Clean water is one of the most precious things in East Africa, and the science of separating…
concepts.example: Islam teaches us to be khalifah - careful guardians of the earth. A scientist who understands materials can protect clean water, avoid waste, and use resources wisely. When you look closely at how water, sand, sugar,…

concepts.title: Islam teaches us to be khalifah - careful guardians of the…
concepts.explanation: Islam teaches us to be khalifah - careful guardians of the earth. A scientist who understands materials can protect clean water, avoid waste, and use resources wisely. When you look closely at how water, sand, sugar, and salt behave, you are also reflecting on the amazing, orderly world that Allah has created. Curiosity and careful observation are beautiful habits for a young Muslim.

explorations.title: What Dissolves in Water?
explorations.context: To find out which everyday materials are soluble (dissolve) and which are insoluble (do not dissolve) in water. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down your guess: Which of these do you think will dissolve - sugar, salt, sand, flour, cooking oil?…
explorations.prompt: Which materials dissolved and made a clear solution?
explorations.answer: Which materials dissolved and made a clear solution? Which materials did not dissolve? What did they do instead - sink or float? Was your prediction correct? It is fine if it was not - that is how we learn!
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which everyday materials are soluble (dissolve) and which are insoluble (do not dissolve) in water. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down your guess: Which of these do you think will dissolve - sugar, salt, sand, flour, cooking oil?…

explorations.title: The Great Dissolving Race (A Fair Test)
explorations.context: To find out whether sugar dissolves faster in warm water or in cool water, using a fair test. Make a Prediction Which cup do you think will win the race - warm or cool? Write your guess and one reason why.
explorations.prompt: What do you predict will happen in The Great Dissolving Race (A Fair Test)?
explorations.answer: Run the investigation, record your observations, and compare them with your prediction.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out whether sugar dissolves faster in warm water or in cool water, using a fair test. Make a Prediction Which cup do you think will win the race - warm or cool? Write your guess and one reason why.

explorations.title: Cleaning Muddy Water (Settle and Filter)
explorations.context: To separate dirt from muddy water using settling and filtering, just like cleaning well water at home. Make a Prediction Do you think letting the water stand still will help? Do you think a cloth will make it clearer? Write your guesses.
explorations.prompt: What happened to the heaviest soil when the water stood still?
explorations.answer: What happened to the heaviest soil when the water stood still? Did filtering through the cloth make the water clearer? What did the cloth catch? Is this water now safe to drink? What one more step is needed?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To separate dirt from muddy water using settling and filtering, just like cleaning well water at home. Make a Prediction Do you think letting the water stand still will help? Do you think a cloth will make it clearer? Write your guesses.

explorations.title: Getting the Salt Back (Evaporation)
explorations.context: To get dissolved salt back out of salty water by evaporation - proving the salt never really disappeared. Make a Prediction When all the water dries up, what will be left in the dish? Write your prediction.
explorations.prompt: Where did the water go? (Hint: into the air as a gas.)
explorations.answer: Where did the water go? (Hint: into the air as a gas.) What was left behind in the dish, and why could it not evaporate too? What does this prove about what happened to the salt when it dissolved?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To get dissolved salt back out of salty water by evaporation - proving the salt never really disappeared. Make a Prediction When all the water dries up, what will be left in the dish? Write your prediction.

explorations.title: Sieving to Separate by Size
explorations.context: To separate a mixture of two different-sized materials using a sieve. Make a Prediction Which material will fall through the holes, and which will stay behind? Why?
explorations.prompt: Which material was small enough to fall through the holes?
explorations.answer: Which material was small enough to fall through the holes? Which material was too big and stayed behind? Why does sieving only work when materials are different sizes? What If? What if both materials were the same size? Would sieving still work? Why not? What if you used a sieve with bigger holes?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To separate a mixture of two different-sized materials using a sieve. Make a Prediction Which material will fall through the holes, and which will stay behind? Why?

methods.title: What Dissolves in Water?
methods.example: To find out which everyday materials are soluble (dissolve) and which are insoluble (do not dissolve) in water. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down your guess: Which of these do you think will dissolve - sugar, salt, sand, flour, cooking oil?…
steps: Fill each of the five cups with the same amount of cool water.
Add one small spoon of sugar to the first cup and stir ten times. Watch closely.
Do the same with salt, sand, flour, and oil - one material per cup, stirring each ten times.
Wait one minute, then look at each cup. Has the material disappeared, sunk, floated, or made the water cloudy?
Record what you see for each cup in the table below.

methods.title: The Great Dissolving Race (A Fair Test)
methods.example: To find out whether sugar dissolves faster in warm water or in cool water, using a fair test. Make a Prediction Which cup do you think will win the race - warm or cool? Write your guess and one reason why.
steps: Fill one glass with warm water and the other with the same amount of cool water.
Add exactly one level spoon of sugar to each glass at the same moment.
Stir each glass the same number of times - for example, ten stirs each.
Watch and time how long the sugar takes to fully disappear in each glass.
Record both times in your notebook.

methods.title: Cleaning Muddy Water (Settle and Filter)
methods.example: To separate dirt from muddy water using settling and filtering, just like cleaning well water at home. Make a Prediction Do you think letting the water stand still will help? Do you think a cloth will make it clearer? Write your guesses.
steps: Stir the soil into the water to make muddy water. Look at how cloudy it is.
Let the jar stand completely still for ten minutes. Watch what the heavy soil does.
Gently pour the clearer water from the top into a second jar, leaving the settled mud behind. This is decanting.
Now fold the clean cloth over the second jar and slowly pour the water through it. This is filtering.
Compare the final water with the muddy water you started with.

methods.title: Getting the Salt Back (Evaporation)
methods.example: To get dissolved salt back out of salty water by evaporation - proving the salt never really disappeared. Make a Prediction When all the water dries up, what will be left in the dish? Write your prediction.
steps: Stir the salt into the warm water until it dissolves completely and the water looks clear.
Pour a thin layer of this salty water into the shallow dish.
Place the dish in a warm, sunny spot where no one will disturb it.
Each day, look at the dish. Watch the water level slowly drop as it evaporates.
When all the water has gone, look closely at what is left behind.

methods.title: Sieving to Separate by Size
methods.example: To separate a mixture of two different-sized materials using a sieve. Make a Prediction Which material will fall through the holes, and which will stay behind? Why?
steps: Look at your mixture and notice that it has two different sizes of material.
Hold the sieve over the bowl and pour the mixture into the sieve.
Gently shake the sieve from side to side.
Watch which material falls through into the bowl, and which stays in the sieve.
Record your results.

workedExamples.title: A Fair Test, Step by Step
workedExamples.prompt: Let us walk through a real fair test together, the way a careful scientist would, so you can see every step. Our question is: Does sugar dissolve faster in hot water or in cold water?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Ask the question. We want to compare hot water and cold water for dissolving sugar. Step 2 - Make a prediction. Sahra predicts the sugar will dissolve faster in the hot water, because she has seen sugar vanish quickly in hot tea. Step 3 - Decide what to change. We change only one thing: the temperature of the water. One cup has warm water (safe to touch, never boiling), the other has cool water. Step 4 - Decide what to keep the same. To be fair, both cups get exactly one level spoon of sugar, exactly the…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: When sugar dissolves in tea, the sugar: (a) disappears forever (b) turns into water (c) breaks into tiny pieces spread through the tea (d) sinks and stays at the bottom
workedExamples.solution: 2. (c) - The sugar breaks into pieces too small to see and spreads through the tea; it is not destroyed.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: A material that dissolves in water is called: (a) insoluble (b) soluble (c) solid (d) magnetic
workedExamples.solution: 3. (b) - A material that dissolves is soluble.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is insoluble in water? (a) sugar (b) salt (c) sand (d) nothing
workedExamples.solution: 4. (c) - Sand is insoluble; it sinks and never dissolves. Sugar and salt do dissolve.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: The clear liquid made when a solid dissolves completely is a: (a) mixture of stones (b) solution (c) sieve (d) filter
workedExamples.solution: 5. (b) - A solution is the clear liquid formed when a solid dissolves completely.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: To separate stones from rice, the best method is: (a) evaporation (b) filtering (c) sieving or picking by size (d) boiling
workedExamples.solution: 6. (c) - Stones and rice are different sizes, so sieving (or picking) separates them.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: To get dissolved salt back from salty water, you should use: (a) a sieve (b) a magnet (c) evaporation (d) a filter
workedExamples.solution: 7. (c) - Dissolved salt is too tiny to sieve or filter, so we evaporate the water and leave the salt behind.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: To take mud out of muddy water, you can: (a) filter it through a cloth (b) use a magnet (c) add more mud (d) freeze it
workedExamples.solution: 8. (a) - Mud is a solid floating in liquid, so filtering through a cloth catches it.

practice.prompt: What is a mixture? (a) One material alone (b) Two or more materials together, each keeping its properties (c) A brand-new material (d) Only liquids
practice.answer: 1. (b) - A mixture is two or more materials together, and each one keeps its own properties.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When sugar dissolves in tea, the sugar: (a) disappears forever (b) turns into water (c) breaks into tiny pieces spread through the tea (d) sinks and stays at the bottom
practice.answer: 2. (c) - The sugar breaks into pieces too small to see and spreads through the tea; it is not destroyed.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A material that dissolves in water is called: (a) insoluble (b) soluble (c) solid (d) magnetic
practice.answer: 3. (b) - A material that dissolves is soluble.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is insoluble in water? (a) sugar (b) salt (c) sand (d) nothing
practice.answer: 4. (c) - Sand is insoluble; it sinks and never dissolves. Sugar and salt do dissolve.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The clear liquid made when a solid dissolves completely is a: (a) mixture of stones (b) solution (c) sieve (d) filter
practice.answer: 5. (b) - A solution is the clear liquid formed when a solid dissolves completely.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: To separate stones from rice, the best method is: (a) evaporation (b) filtering (c) sieving or picking by size (d) boiling
practice.answer: 6. (c) - Stones and rice are different sizes, so sieving (or picking) separates them.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: To get dissolved salt back from salty water, you should use: (a) a sieve (b) a magnet (c) evaporation (d) a filter
practice.answer: 7. (c) - Dissolved salt is too tiny to sieve or filter, so we evaporate the water and leave the salt behind.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: To take mud out of muddy water, you can: (a) filter it through a cloth (b) use a magnet (c) add more mud (d) freeze it
practice.answer: 8. (a) - Mud is a solid floating in liquid, so filtering through a cloth catches it.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Sugar dissolves faster when the water is: (a) colder (b) warmer (c) not stirred (d) in a bigger cup only
practice.answer: 9. (b) - Warmer water makes a solid dissolve faster.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In a fair test you should change: (a) everything at once (b) nothing (c) only one thing at a time (d) things randomly
practice.answer: 10. (c) - A fair test means changing only one thing at a time.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Cooking oil in water will: (a) dissolve quickly (b) float in a separate layer (c) turn into salt (d) disappear
practice.answer: 11. (b) - Oil is insoluble and floats in a separate layer on the water.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Filtering muddy water makes it look clean, but before drinking it you must still: (a) freeze it (b) boil it (c) add salt (d) sieve it
practice.answer: 12. (b) - Filtering removes visible dirt, but germs remain, so the water must be boiled.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: What Dissolves in Water?
activities.materials: 5 clear cups or glasses of water; A small spoon of each: sugar, salt, sand (or soil), flour, cooking oil; A stirring spoon; Your notebook and a pencil
steps: Fill each of the five cups with the same amount of cool water.
Add one small spoon of sugar to the first cup and stir ten times. Watch closely.
Do the same with salt, sand, flour, and oil - one material per cup, stirring each ten times.
Wait one minute, then look at each cup. Has the material disappeared, sunk, floated, or made the water cloudy?
Record what you see for each cup in the table below.

activities.title: The Great Dissolving Race (A Fair Test)
activities.materials: 2 clear glasses the same size; Warm water (safe to touch) and cool water; 2 level spoons of sugar; A clock or a way to count slowly; Your notebook
steps: Fill one glass with warm water and the other with the same amount of cool water.
Add exactly one level spoon of sugar to each glass at the same moment.
Stir each glass the same number of times - for example, ten stirs each.
Watch and time how long the sugar takes to fully disappear in each glass.
Record both times in your notebook.

activities.title: Cleaning Muddy Water (Settle and Filter)
activities.materials: A cup of clean water mixed with a spoon of soil or sand (your 'muddy water'); 2 clean jars or cups; A clean cotton cloth or a coffee filter; A spoon
steps: Stir the soil into the water to make muddy water. Look at how cloudy it is.
Let the jar stand completely still for ten minutes. Watch what the heavy soil does.
Gently pour the clearer water from the top into a second jar, leaving the settled mud behind. This is decanting.
Now fold the clean cloth over the second jar and slowly pour the water through it. This is filtering.
Compare the final water with the muddy water you started with.

activities.title: Getting the Salt Back (Evaporation)
activities.materials: A spoon of salt; A cup of warm water; A shallow dish or plate (dark colours show salt best); A sunny windowsill or warm, safe spot; Patience - this takes 1 to 3 days!
steps: Stir the salt into the warm water until it dissolves completely and the water looks clear.
Pour a thin layer of this salty water into the shallow dish.
Place the dish in a warm, sunny spot where no one will disturb it.
Each day, look at the dish. Watch the water level slowly drop as it evaporates.
When all the water has gone, look closely at what is left behind.

activities.title: Sieving to Separate by Size
activities.materials: A kitchen sieve or a clean cloth with small holes; A mixture of two sizes, such as flour with a few dry beans in it, or rice mixed with a spoon of salt; A bowl to catch the small pieces; Your notebook
steps: Look at your mixture and notice that it has two different sizes of material.
Hold the sieve over the bowl and pour the mixture into the sieve.
Gently shake the sieve from side to side.
Watch which material falls through into the bowl, and which stays in the sieve.
Record your results.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: Filtering removes bits of dirt you can see, but it does NOT remove tiny germs. Water must always be boiled after filtering before it is safe to drink. Boiling and clean water protect your family from sickness. This is science that keeps you healthy.

terms: A solid is floating in a liquid
A solid is dissolved in a liquid
A material that can dissolve (sugar, salt).
A material that does not dissolve (sand, oil).
To pull a mixture back into its different materials.
A tool with small holes that separates materials by size.
A cloth or paper that lets liquid through but catches solid bits.
When a liquid slowly turns into a gas and floats into the air.
When heavy solids sink to the bottom of a still liquid.
commonMistakes: It is still there, in pieces too small to see. Evaporation brings it back.
A sieve can separate salt from salty water.
Dissolved salt is far too tiny to catch.
Filtered water is safe to drink.
Oil dissolves if you stir hard.
Oil is insoluble and always separates.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you have learned to solve these real problems from daily life.
realProblems.answer: 1. Let the muddy water settle so heavy soil sinks; decant (pour off) the clearer water; filter it through a clean cloth; then boil it to kill germs. Now it is safe.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. Let the muddy water settle so heavy soil sinks; decant (pour off) the clearer water; filter it through a clean cloth; then boil it to kill germs. Now it is safe.

realProblems.prompt: After heavy rain near Hargeisa, the well water is brown and muddy. Describe the steps you would take to make it clean and safe to drink.
realProblems.answer: 2. Sieve the rice, or pick the stones out by hand. This works because the stones and rice are different sizes and the stones are heavier and harder.

realProblems.prompt: Grandmother finds small stones mixed into the bariis (rice). What is the easiest way to separate them, and why does it work?
realProblems.answer: 3. Evaporate the water by leaving the salty water in a warm, sunny place. The water turns to gas and floats away, leaving the salt behind, because dissolved salt cannot evaporate.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan accidentally stirred salt into a jug of water. He wants the salt back. How can he get it, and why will that method work?

realProblems.prompt: Halima wants her sugar to dissolve quickly in cold water. Give her two tips to make it dissolve faster.
realProblems.answer: 5. Let the sand settle to the bottom and pour off the water, or filter the mixture through a cloth to catch the sand. Sand is insoluble and heavier, so it separates easily.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: In your own words, what is a mixture?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1. A mixture is two or more materials put together where each material keeps its own properties and can usually be separated again.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What does it mean when we say sugar has dissolved?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2. Dissolving means the solid breaks into pieces too tiny to see and spreads evenly through the liquid, forming a solution. It is still there.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give one example of a soluble material and one example of an insoluble material.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3. Soluble example: sugar or salt. Insoluble example: sand or oil. (Any correct pair.)

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does the whole cup of tea taste sweet after the sugar dissolves?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4. Because the tiny sugar pieces spread evenly all through the water, so every part of the cup is sweet - not just the bottom.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the three things that make a solid dissolve faster.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5. Heat (warmer water), stirring, and smaller grain size all make a solid dissolve faster.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why can you not use a sieve to separate salt from salty water?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6. Because dissolved salt is broken into pieces far too small for any sieve's holes to catch; the salt passes straight through.

questions.question: What is a mixture?
options: Two or more materials together, each keeping its properties
questions.answer: Two or more materials together, each keeping its properties
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A mixture is two or more materials together, and each one keeps its own properties.

questions.question: When sugar dissolves in tea, the sugar:
options: breaks into tiny pieces spread through the tea
sinks and stays at the bottom
questions.answer: breaks into tiny pieces spread through the tea
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: The sugar breaks into pieces too small to see and spreads through the tea; it is not destroyed.

questions.question: A material that dissolves in water is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A material that dissolves is soluble.

questions.question: Which of these is insoluble in water?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Sand is insoluble; it sinks and never dissolves. Sugar and salt do dissolve.

questions.question: The clear liquid made when a solid dissolves completely is a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A solution is the clear liquid formed when a solid dissolves completely.

questions.question: To separate stones from rice, the best method is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Stones and rice are different sizes, so sieving (or picking) separates them.

questions.question: To get dissolved salt back from salty water, you should use:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Dissolved salt is too tiny to sieve or filter, so we evaporate the water and leave the salt behind.

questions.question: To take mud out of muddy water, you can:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Mud is a solid floating in liquid, so filtering through a cloth catches it.

questions.question: Sugar dissolves faster when the water is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Warmer water makes a solid dissolve faster.

questions.question: In a fair test you should change:
options: only one thing at a time
questions.answer: only one thing at a time
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A fair test means changing only one thing at a time.

questions.question: Cooking oil in water will:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Oil is insoluble and floats in a separate layer on the water.

questions.question: Filtering muddy water makes it look clean, but before drinking it you must still:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 2 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Filtering removes visible dirt, but germs remain, so the water must be boiled.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to:
I can explain what a mixture is and give examples of mixtures from your own home.
I can describe what it means for a material to dissolve, and use the word solution correctly.
I can predict and test which materials dissolve in water and which do not.
I can name and describe four ways to separate mixtures: sieving, filtering, evaporation, and letting things settle (decanting).
I can choose the best separation method for a given mixture and explain why it works.
I can plan and carry out a fair test, changing only one thing at a time.
I can record what you observe carefully and honestly, and draw a sensible conclusion.
